nativeRange.habitat: Ingen's Seahorse is found in subtidal reef-associated habitats from the surface down to about 60 m depth, though it is most commonly encountered between 3 and 18 m. It anchors itself to a variety of structures, including gorgonians, black coral trees, mangroves, seagrass meadows, rocky reefs, kelp, and eelgrass beds. The species has been reported to be nocturnal.

diet.notes: Primarily frozen or live mysid shrimp; also has been documented accepting enriched brine shrimp (Artemia), amphipods, and small crustaceans from live rock. Captive-bred individuals tend to accept frozen mysis more readily than wild-caught specimens. Generally advised to feed two or more small portions per day. Slow feeders that can be outcompeted by faster-moving tankmates.

summary: The largest seahorse of the Eastern Pacific, Ingen's Seahorse (H. ingens) is generally considered an advanced species best kept in a dedicated species tank with ample hitching posts, gentle flow, and multiple daily feedings of frozen mysis shrimp.
careNotes:
Ingen's Seahorse is widely regarded as an advanced-care species best kept in a dedicated aquarium. Fast-moving or aggressive tankmates have been known to outcompete seahorses for food and cause chronic stress, so a species-only setup — or one limited to similarly calm, slow-moving fish such as pipefish and docile gobies — is generally advised.

A tank of at least 35 gallons (133 L) is recommended for a pair; keepers commonly suggest adding approximately 20 gallons per additional pair. Gentle, low-to-moderate water flow is essential, as the strong circulation typical of reef setups tends to stress this species. Ample hitching posts — gorgonians, artificial branches, and macroalgae — are important for natural resting behavior.

Temperature guidance varies somewhat across sources: aquarium husbandry guidance generally recommends keeping water temperature between 20 and 26°C, with many experienced keepers suggesting the lower portion of that range (around 21–23°C) to reduce the risk of Vibrio bacterial infections. Target pH 8.1–8.4, alkalinity 8–12 dKH, and specific gravity 1.021–1.025.

Adult size varies considerably — captive individuals typically reach 12–19 cm, while the species can grow to 30 cm in the wild. Ingen's Seahorse holds IUCN Vulnerable status and is listed on CITES Appendix II; captive-bred specimens are strongly preferred, as wild-caught individuals tend to have difficulty accepting prepared foods and generally show poorer long-term survival.

The species is sold under several names, including Pacific Seahorse, Giant Seahorse, and Gigantes Seahorse. Coloration is highly variable; individuals have been recorded in green, reddish-maroon, gray, yellow, gold, and brown.
breedingNotes: Ovoviviparous; males carry eggs in a ventral brood pouch. Gestation has been reported at 14–15 days, with brood sizes of up to 2,000 reported (though 60–400 is more typical). Sexual maturity tends to occur around 3–8 months of age. Courtship involves elaborate displays lasting several days. Captive breeding has been documented and is achievable, though fry require live zooplankton (rotifers, copepods) and frequent feedings. Generally classified as a difficult species to breed in captivity.

additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (13–30 cm), lifespan (3–5 years), breeding (brood sizes up to 2,000, maturity 3–6 months), diet (Artemia and mysids documented). Confirmed IUCN Vulnerable status and 50% population decline 2007–2012.

fish.breedingNotes: Males carry the eggs in a ventral brood pouch, with gestation typically lasting 14 to 15 days. Brood sizes most commonly fall between 60 and 400 young, though broods of up to 2,000 have been reported. Fry require live zooplankton such as rotifers and copepods, fed frequently. Captive breeding is achievable but is generally considered demanding.
